data.title: Sea: Steep Investments Necessary To Defend Leadership - Maintain Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Sea Limited (SE) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating upon a further moderation to the $108s, with the much-needed meltdown already triggering a notably improved margin of safety from the cheaper valuations and the expanded capital appreciation prospects.

Combined with the robust performance metrics observed in each of the three segments and the synergistic nature of the eCommerce/fintech segments, I was of the opinion that the well-diversified company's investment thesis had improved drastically, despite the falling knife risks.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the SE stock after the materialization of a double bottom, thanks to the improved margin of safety from the recent selloff and the consequently cheaper valuations.

This is significantly aided by the diversified company's high growth top-line prospects and the still resilient profit margins despite the intensified growth initiatives, as the management attempts to drive market share gains, supported by the richer free cash flow generation and the healthier balance sheet.

SE Defends eCommerce Leadership In SEA

For now, SE's correction has occurred, albeit steeper than expected, as the stock lost nearly all of their Q4'24-Q3'25 gains to retest the prior support levels of $80s by the time of writing, with a similar pullback also observed in many of its diversified commerce peers in varying degrees.

This is worsened by the market overreacting to SE's supposedly drastic increase in expenses and provision for credit losses for the Monee segment, with it contributing to the steep one-day correction by -16.5% post FQ4'25 earnings call.

For now, I am of the opinion that the selloff has been overly done indeed, as the market notably discounts SE's undeniably robust performance metrics across the eCommerce and gaming segments.

1. Monee

For example, the source of the pessimism is attributed to Monee's drastic increase in expenses, which contributed to the digital financial services' deteriorating adjusted EBITDA margins of 23.9% in FQ4'25 (-2.1 points QoQ/-4.8 YoY/-6.6 from the FY2023 highs of 30.5%).

The deterioration in its profit margins may be jarring in comparison to the segment's accelerating top-line growth to $1.1B in FQ4'25 (+54.3% YoY) and the growing consumer/SME loans to $9.2B (+80.4%), as the SE management seemingly focuses on market share gains at a time of intensifying fintech competition (including from Grab Holdings (GRAB)) while putting their bottom-line performance on the back seat.

Given that Monee used to be a bottom-line driver in FY2023 before the eCommerce segment turned profitable, I can understand why the market has overreacted indeed, especially since their provision for credit losses has grown alongside the loan book to $393.1M (+66.7% YoY), with it presenting the mixed optics to its digital financial services' performance metrics.

Otherwise, readers only need to focus on their moderating non-performing loans ratio of 1.1% in FQ4'25 (inline QoQ/-0.1 points YoY) to understand that Monee's loan book remains extremely healthy thus far.

This is especially since SE has reported great success in growing the use-cases of Monee beyond the Shopee ecosystem, as observed in the off-Shopee SPayLater loans growing by +300% YoY, while attracting 5.8M of unique first-time borrowers in FQ4'25 and growing their active credit users to 37M (+40% YoY).

These developments lend credence to the segment's high-growth prospects, albeit with further near-term bottom-line noise likely as the management hints at further "progress growing our products and services beyond credit from digital banking to insurance and more."

2. Garena/Shopee

I am of the opinion that Monee's deteriorating adj. EBITDA margins have been well balanced by SE's gaming segment, Garena, expanding adj. EBITDA margins (to bookings) of 54.1% in FQ4'25 (+0.8 points YoY) and 56.1% in FY2025 (+0.3 points YoY/+5.2 from FY2023 levels of 50.9%).

Much of the expanding profitability is thanks to the improved operating leverage as they grow their quarterly paying users to 58M (+15% YoY) and their paying user ratio to 9.2% (+1 point YoY), significantly aided by the expanding bookings per user at $1.06 (+20.4% YoY).

At the same time, Shopee has further entrenched itself as the eCommerce market leader, as observed in the expanding regional share to 52% in 2024 (+4 points YoY) despite the intensifying competition from ByteDance's (BDNCE) TikTok, Alibaba's (BABA) Lazada, and Amazon (AMZN), among others.

The same has been observed in Shopee's growing orders by +30.5% YoY and the higher Gross Merchandise Volumes by +28.6% YoY, all of which contribute to the segment's expanding marketplace revenues of $4.3B (+34.3% YoY).

Much of SE's success is attributed to their diversified logistics approach for different markets, such as instant/same-day delivery services for urban areas and economical shipping for suburban areas, allowing them to drive improved penetration across buyers and sellers - all of which feeding its vertically integrated eCommerce, logistics, and fintech growth flywheel.

This is on top of the management taking a leaf out of AMZN's Amazon Prime playbook, with the introduction of Shopee VIP membership program being well received, as observed in the accelerating member growth to over 7M by the end of the 2025 year, despite the recent launch in Q1'25.

Given the relatively low penetration rate of ~1.7% at 400M of its active buyers as of 2025, I am of the opinion that the eCommerce segment has further room for growth.

This is especially since VIP members typically spend more at higher frequency, allowing Shopee to incrementally gain market share in an increasingly competitive eCommerce market:

And what we are doing in 2026 is essentially to reach out those users to convert them to our platforms and hence, enhance the overall -- the MTUs -- the MAUs for our platforms. So that's kind of a broader theme of what we are doing in South Asia. (Seeking Alpha)

3. Mixed FY2026 Guidance

Part of the selloff may also be attributed to SE's mixed FY2026 guidance with Shopee GMV growth of +25% YoY albeit with a "full year adjusted EBITDA no lower than that of 2025 in absolute dollar terms," with it implying a deteriorating adj EBITDA margins.

When compared against the overall company's adj EBITDA margins of 11.4% in FQ4'25 (-0.4 points YoY) and 14.8% in FY2025 (+2.9 points YoY/+5.7 from FY2023 levels of 9.1%, the first year that they are profitable on adj EBITDA basis), it is unsurprising that the market has turned pessimistic.

These developments imply SE's intensified efforts to stave off the growing eCommerce/fintech competition from other players, as Momentum Works also report TikTok Shop's doubled GMV in Southeast Asia [SEA] in 2025.

While their "calculations also suggest Shopee remained ahead in order and parcel volumes – but the gap was clearly narrowing across the region" and TikTok shop expected to "catches up with Shopee in parcel volume" in 2026, I can understand the market's decision to de-risk and sell first.

This is especially since it remains to be seen how long SE's intensified investments and the consequently, impacted adj EBITDA margins may last.

The same caution has also embedded in the consensus latest forward estimates, with SE expected to report a mixed top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +20.9%/+27.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +17.3%/+33.3% while notably decelerating from the 2Y historical growth profile of +32.5%/+101.2% (the period of time that they are adj EPS profitable), respectively.

These reasons may also be why SE has been notably downgraded to FY2025 EV/Sales valuations of 1.95x and FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $44.87B and the FY2025 revenues of $22.93B along the current stock prices of $87.82 and the FY2025 adj EPS of $3.17.

This is down from the recent highs of 4.56x/41.22x and the 5Y mean of 5.49x/38.21x, albeit still somewhat inflated against the sector median of 1.28x/15.95x, respectively.

Given their high growth, top-line cadence, I am of the opinion that SE is extremely compelling here, as similarly observed in my estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 1.11x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $44.87B and the consensus FY2028 revenue estimates of $40.1B.

Based on the FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x and the consensus ajd EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +27.5%, the stock is still attractive at my estimated 3Y PEG ratio of 1.00x, compared to the sector median of 1.65x.

When comparing SE's FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x and my estimated 3Y PEG ratio of 1.00x to its well-diversified commerce peers, including:

  • AMZN at 27x/1.52x (diversified, including cloud and advertising),
  • MercadoLibre (MELI) at 31.03x/0.80x (diversified, including fintech), and
  • BABA at 22.62x/4.52x (diversified, including cloud and entertainment), respectively,

it is apparent that SE's intensified growth initiatives and the consequently downgraded consensus adj EPS estimates have triggered near-term noise to its investment thesis, albeit with the steep correction bringing the stock nearer to its peer group P/E mean of ~27x.

Thanks to the richer Free Cash Flow generation of $4.51B (+52.3% YoY), readers may want to note that SE already reports an extremely healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $8.72B (+54.8% YoY) and a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of -2.54x in FY2025 (given the higher cash than debt).

When compared to AMZN at -0.39x in FY2025, MELI at 1.04x in FY2025, and BABA at -1.72x in FQ3'25 (given the higher cash than debt), I am of the opinion that SE remains well capitalized to strategically invest in their growth opportunities despite the near-term margin headwinds.

SE Remains A Great Buy, After A Double Bottom

For now, it remains to be seen when the bulls may defend a bottom, as SE lost -55.9% of its value since the 52 week highs while consistently running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

As a result of the steep selloff, it is unsurprising that SE has already entered oversold territories, as observed in the low RSIs and trading volumes, as the stock retests the August 2022/Q2'23 resistance levels of $80s by the time of writing.

Based on the FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x (near to the peer group mean of ~27x) and the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $6.57, there remains an excellent upside potential of +107% to my long-term price target of $181.90, with it apparent that the correction has been a boon for those looking to add.

As a result of the improved margin of safety from the recent selloff, their high-growth top-line prospects, and the cheap FY2028 EV/Sales valuations, along with their ability to incrementally invest in their growth initiatives thanks to the richer free cash flow generation and the healthier balance sheet, I am maintaining my buy rating for the SE stock.

This is preferably after the bulls defend a double bottom at the $80s, barring which, a further retracement to the next support levels of the $70s is not overly bearish, given the currently pessimistic stock market sentiments.

data.title: Kearny Financial: Improving Profitability, But Still Not Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to my previous analysis of Kearny Financial (KRNY), the New Jersey-based holding company for Kearny Bank. When I first wrote about KRNY in early October of last year, I rated the stock as a hold. While the company was trading at a low valuation, I had concerns at the time about declining net interest income, overall uneven earnings trends, and relatively low net interest margin. Fast forward to today, and Kearny has posted its first two quarterly results for FY 2026, and its stock is up almost 17% since that previous article was written.

Not all of the issues that I mentioned in October have been fixed completely, but the company’s bottom line appears to be on a stronger trajectory for the second half of its fiscal year. Credit quality appears to be solid, and the bank could be a takeover candidate for a larger bank looking for access to the New Jersey market. Unfortunately, there is still just not enough quality in the operations of the company for me to raise my opinion of its stock, but I would say that it is closer to a buy than it was last fall.

Company Overview

Kearny Bank is a small-cap community bank that operates 40 branch locations in New Jersey and metropolitan New York City, with the bulk of those being in the Garden State, as shown below. KRNY has a current market cap of $516.3 million. The bank has a history that dates back over 140 years. While much of the bank’s growth in assets over the last 15 years can be attributed to acquisitions, it has been almost six years now since it was involved in M&A activity.

At the end of 2025, Kearny Financial reported ownership of $7.6 billion in assets, $5.8 billion of which were comprised of outstanding loans. (The company’s fiscal year ends on June 30, so the most recent quarterly report was for Q2 2026.) A breakdown of that loan portfolio is shown below. Over 55% of the bank’s loans are connected to properties in New Jersey. An additional 32.8% correspond to assets in New York. The size of the loan portfolio has decreased 0.8% when compared to Q2 2025.

The largest segment of the bank’s loans is commercial multifamily loans at 45.5%, a 1.1% decrease from last October’s report. Residential mortgages have stayed consistent over the last two quarters at 30.1%. Commercial real estate has the bulk of the remaining loans outstanding at 17.2%. While the relatively low amount of CRE reduces some of the risk taken on by the bank, it also lowers its average loan yield, which was 4.67% at the end of Q2 2026. The most recent report from the FDIC states that the average loan yield for a bank of Kearny’s size is 6.56%. KRNY’s underperformance here is something the company has committed to working on in the year ahead, but improvements will not come quickly.

Kearny Financial has a current quarterly dividend payout of $0.11, an amount that has not changed since Q4 2021. Its stock currently yields 5.36%, well above the median for other peers from its sector. As I pointed out in my initial analysis, KRNY gets low marks in dividend safety, as its payout ratio is over twice the norm for the last 12 months.

Analysis of Kearny Financial

Kearny Financial last reported its quarterly results on January 22. KRNY earned $0.15 per diluted share, matching its results from Q1 2026. Considering the company earned $0.42 per share in FY 2025, these results from the first two quarters put Kearny on track for overall earnings growth of over 42%, year-over-year. However, KRNY has a long way to go before it can match its results from FY 2022, when it reported a profit of $0.92 per share.

Beyond just the improvement in profits, what has caught my attention are trends that are moving in the right direction under the surface. Below are several other highlights from Q2 2026.

  • Net interest margin expanded by four basis points to 2.14%. This marked the fifth straight quarter of margin improvement dating back to Q2 2025. However, this number is still well below the average for a regional bank.
  • Net interest income, which was a point of concern from my previous article, increased 16.4% in Q2 compared to the previous year. For the first six months of FY 2026, net interest income is up 16.3% when compared to the same time period in FY 2025.
  • Non-performing assets decreased 20.6% to $51.3 million, or 0.67% of total assets, showing improvement after a large increase in Q1 2026 that was related to one loan that has since been repaid.
  • Net charge offs decreased from 0.07% to 0.05%, as shown below.

The bank’s balance of loans to deposits has been improving in the first six months of FY 2026. Total loans outstanding have decreased slightly from $5.81 billion to $5.75 billion, a decline of about 1%. Total deposits have grown only 0.6%, but much more significant improvements have been made in the category that is most valuable to the bank: non-interest-bearing deposits. This type of deposit has increased $45.1 million, or 7.8%, in the first six months of the current fiscal year. This trend, along with an overall increase in the bank’s loan yield, should help continue the much needed improvements in the bank’s net interest margin.

When I first wrote about Kearny Financial, its stock was trading at only 0.68x its tangible book value. Today that discount is 0.78x. However, the tangible book value for the company is increasing at a pedestrian rate, less than 1% in the last quarter. The performance metrics below help explain much of the reason for the discount, as the typical bank trades around 1.4x or 1.5x TBV per share.

National Average

Q2 2026

Q1 2026

Q4 2025

Q3 2025

Return on Assets

1.31%

0.50%

0.50%

0.35%

0.35%

Return on Equity

11.88%

5.01%

5.10%

3.64%

3.57%

Efficiency Ratio

58.58%

71.64%

72.71%

75.66%

78.72%

The biggest issue I have with the company is its performance metrics, which are well below the national averages for a bank of its size. I could be more inclined to overlook them if they were all making steady progress, but unfortunately, they are not. After a significant leap forward at the start of the current fiscal year, return on assets was flat in Q2. Return on equity actually declined by nine basis points. The efficiency ratio is improving, but it too is well above average. In short, the bank still has significant work to do to make up the gap between its performance and higher-performing peers.

Risks to Consider

The limited geographic footprint for the bank certainly presents some limitations for its operations. The company is especially dependent on the economy in the state of New Jersey and the area around New York City. There are added concerns that the new mayor in NYC will be less friendly to businesses and place increasing tax burdens on its citizens. The company may have alleviated some of these concerns in its last earnings presentation, where it noted its limited exposure to multi-family loans tied to rent-controlled properties in New York, as shown below.

The bank is also exposed to almost $1 billion in commercial real estate loans. While Kearny may not be as exposed to non-residential CRE as significantly as some other small-cap regional banks that I have covered in the last year, this is still another added risk for its overall future health. The bank has its highest percentage of CRE exposure to retail CRE at 27% of the total loan segment. The highest risk belongs to office CRE, which comprises 13.5% of this segment. Increases in remote work have impacted occupancy rates nationwide, and conversions to other types of properties can be costly and inefficient.

Conclusion

In the conclusion of my last article, I stated that KRNY's discounted multiple to its tangible book value was a reflection of the current state of the operations of the bank. I also said that if the bank is able to stay on the expected pace of improvements, it could be more attractive as a potential investment for both individual investors as well as a larger bank that may want to expand into the northern half of New Jersey.

Fast forward to today, and the bank seems to be slowly attacking those areas where I said it needed to improve. Earnings of $0.69 per share that are expected for FY 2026 are predicted to grow 30.0% to $0.89 per share in FY 2027. However, there are only two analysts that cover Kearny Financial at the current time, so these estimates are not quite as meaningful as they would be for a larger bank. The graph below shows just how significant the next four quarters could be for its investors, but some of this expected improvement is already priced into the stock that trades at a forward PE of over 11.

Kearny Financial does not have the usual profile of a bank that I consider to be a buy because of some of its low operational metrics mentioned earlier, but the trends are promising, even if slow-moving. The price-to-book value multiple is attractive and could catch the eye of an acquiring bank, even with its current issues. Nonetheless, I am sticking with my Hold rating for the company for now, with a plan to revisit toward the end of its fiscal year.

data.title: LegalZoom: EBIT Needs To Grow
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about LegalZoom (LZ) previously with a hold rating, as organic growth was weak and I didn’t see any strong catalyst that could turn things around in the near term. I still think a hold rating is the right view. Subscription growth accelerated meaningfully, ARPU turned positive, and management commentary around retention and DIFM was encouraging. Those are real improvements. But I do not think the setup is clean enough for an upgrade yet.

4Q25 earnings

This was actually quite a good quarter for LZ. Revenue growth accelerated 500 bps sequentially, from 13% to 18%, with the mix also leaning more toward recurring revenue. Subscription revenue was $130.9 million, up 20% y/y, while transaction revenue was $59.3 million, up 12% y/y. Gross margin also expanded, by 100 bps y/y to 68%, but so have expenses across all key lines. Sales and marketing went up quite a bit to $63 million (vs. $47.5 million in Q4 2024); technology and development increased to $19.5 million (vs. $16.7 million), and general and administrative expenses increased to $33.5 million (vs. $31 million). This led to a delta in topline and bottomline growth, wherein EBIT fell ~$500 million to $12.6 million, and net income fell to $6.1 million (vs. $12.9 million).

Lots of good developments, but more is needed

Most of the weaknesses/concerns I found last quarter, like weak ARPU, retention, and underlying formation activity, have faded. LZ reported a blowout quarter with subscription growth accelerating to 20% y/y (vs. 13% y/y in 3Q25), subscription units up 10%, and ARPU turned positive (1% y/y growth). As for retention, this was noted to have improved in compliance subscriptions, especially in the younger cohorts. This particular point about younger cohorts matters a lot because it suggests newer customers are finding more value in the product from the start. This points to a healthier funnel, wherein customers are coming in, seeing the value sooner, and potentially adopting more modules earlier in the lifecycle. The one area I would still be careful about is underlying formation activity. For what it is worth, reported business formations did grow by 17% y/y, but management also said that growth was driven by Formation Nation and the partner channel. Excluding BOIR and Formation Nation, this is only up 5%, which is better than last year, but not really a strong sign of growth.

LZ's efforts in concierge and the broader DIFM push are also starting to show up tangibly. I wrote before that DIFM could become a growth driver, and this quarter gave a bit more support for that view. Referencing management comments from the call, concierge services (including work like reinstatement, dissolution, and conversion) are being sold both through the formation funnel (new adds) and into the installed base. Why this matters is because compliance needs are fluid (things change very frequently). Most businesses may like to think they can handle compliance themselves, which is true since the business is small at the start. But as the business evolves over time, things change, and staying compliant becomes harder to manage (especially if you become a multinational company). As such, this concierge service has very tangible value benefits, in my view.

What I think is bullish here is that this opens up two growth paths. First, LZ can attach these higher-value services to new customers coming through the funnel (they can pitch using case studies and probably bundle it to make it more attractive). Second, it can go back into the installed base and sell more to existing customers as their needs become more complex or as they fall out of compliance. In other words, growth is no longer just about adding new formations. It is also about monetizing the installed base better over time. Note that these are higher-value services too, at >$1,100 per year on average (which is close to 4x subscription ARPU).

Valuation

In November, the stock traded at 8x NTM EBITDA, and I must say it is surprising that valuation has de-rated so sharply now to just 5x NTM EBITDA. It appears that the market is confident that AI will replace LZ (triggered by Anthropic). There is a huge debate about whether AI can really replace software, but in the case of LZ, I think that fear has gone too far. AI is clearly changing how customers begin the process (no doubts here), but that is not the same as replacing LZ. What these tools are good at is helping users search and review. The harder part is the last mile, which is actually getting the formation done, staying compliant, fixing problems when a business falls out of compliance, and connecting customers to real legal help when needed. That’s why I think LZ has real value here.

From this perspective, LZ should at least trade back to 8x (recovering the entire wipeout since November), and that yields very attractive upside. I would say that I am inching towards upgrading LZ to a buy simply because valuation has gotten so cheap and the positive developments we have seen are really encouraging. What I need to see here is EBIT has to grow in line with revenue, not in opposite directions. New business formations also need to pick up speed to make the growth equation more attractive. Until then, this is a pass for me.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for LZ. This was a much better quarter, and I think the stock is starting to look interesting again given stronger subscription trends, better ARPU, and a cheaper valuation. I also think the market may be too negative on the AI risk, as LZ still has value in compliance, concierge, and the last-mile execution piece. But I am not going to turn bullish yet. EBIT needs to grow more in line with revenue, and core formation activity still needs to show clearer acceleration before I would upgrade.

data.title: South Korea Private Equity Deal Activity Falls In 2025
data.description:
Global private equity and venture capital deal value in South Korea totaled $8.58 billion, down 38.8% from the prior year and the lowest annual figure since at least 2020.

The number of transactions fell to 446 from 582 in 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

South Korea's overall M&A activity slowed significantly from December 2024 through June 2025, Jin-Won Kim, a partner at KPMG in South Korea, told Market Intelligence. During the period, martial law was declared, the president was subsequently impeached and a period of leadership uncertainty affected the country's stability.

Since June 2025, transaction volumes have shown signs of recovery, Kim said, with global private equity firms and local investors now actively exploring M&A opportunities.

The second half of 2025 saw notable private equity investments in the country, led by large global managers, including The Carlyle Group Inc.'s (CG) pending purchase of KFC Korea Co. Ltd., KKR & Co. Inc.'s (KKR) acquisition of Samhwa P&T Co. Ltd. and EQT AB (publ)'s (EQBBF) acquisition of Drama & Co.

KPMG's Kim observed that local private equity players "have been a little bit slow because they are watching how government or regulators have tried to control local private equity over the last five or six months."

South Korea intends to tighten private equity rules after local firm MBK Partners was placed under investigation by government prosecutors over a debt issue involving its investment in supermarket chain Homeplus.

Even as deal activity declined in 2024, South Korea was among Asia-Pacific markets that have attracted the most private equity investment.

The country had the highest private equity penetration rate in the region, at 6.81% as of late November 2025. The measurement refers to the percentage of total private companies in a market that have private equity or venture capital investment.

Top sector

The technology, media and telecom sector led private equity transactions in 2025, attracting $2.80 billion in private equity investment. Deal count in TMT also led all sectors, at 165.

Notable TMT deals announced in 2025 include the pending $907.3 million acquisition of a 37.6% stake in AI infrastructure solutions company Douzone Bizon Co. Ltd. by EQT Partners Korea Co. Ltd. and BPEA Private Equity Fund IX. In February, less than four months later, EQT Partners Korea proposed to acquire an additional 57.69% stake in Douzone Bizon in a roughly $1.63 billion deal that would take the company private, according to Market Intelligence data.

"AI infrastructure is currently attracting substantial national interest and investment," said Jinyoung Kim, research fellow at the Korea Capital Market Institute. "AI-enabled SaaS is also widely viewed as a high-potential segment."

Top deals

In South Korea's largest private equity deal of 2025, Seoul-based Hahn & Co. completed the 2.6 trillion won acquisition of SK Specialty Co. Ltd., the specialty gases unit of industrial conglomerate SK Inc., The Korea Economic Daily reported. The SK Group retained a 15% stake in the business following the deal.

The South Korean government supports capital flows into sectors that enhance long-term economic competitiveness. "Startups and companies operating in advanced industries are therefore likely to benefit from this policy direction," Kim with Korea Capital said.

Outlook

In 2026, private equity funds are set to play a prominent role in South Korea's M&A market, with a strong focus on acquiring conglomerate carve-outs, according to Jihun Kim, executive director at DC Advisory.

As major conglomerates grapple with liquidity pressures and declining profitability — particularly in sectors such as chemicals — they are increasingly divesting noncore assets. This wave of divestments is creating attractive opportunities for both large and small private equity funds to acquire and restructure these businesses, DC Advisory's Kim said, adding that the trend is expected to drive significant deal activity.

Another potential sector for deals is the "K-beauty" sector, which shows outward expansion into markets like the US, marked by overseas interest in South Korean cosmetics and skincare products. A top deal in 2025 was Goodai Global Inc.'s proposed 600 billion-won acquisition of Seorin Co. Ltd.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: After Scouting The S&P 500 For 5-Year Losers, These 5 Stocks Stood Out As Possible Winners
data.description:
I typically write about "risk management." The science and art of playing defense alongside my investment offense. Because I think so few writers do. Collectively, the nearly 20-year run in US mega-cap stocks, with only brief dips to buy along the way, has truly spoiled a lot of investors. How else do we explain the constant bombardment of "buy now" articles, buy ratings on single stocks, and general happy-talk in investing circles? It has really run amok, particularly since the pandemic six years ago.

I do not know what path stocks and bonds will take going forward. No one does. But I know I'm prepared for a very wide range of possible outcomes. That's where I think the masses get left out. Because everyone's trying to sell them the dream. Instead of teaching them to be their own portfolio manager. Foremost in that "job description"—learn to manage risk.

I'm taking a break from that "beat" today. Sort of. I'm looking at stocks, specifically large-cap types, most of whom are part of the S&P 500. And I'm looking at them with a specific objective in mind, one I frankly do not spend much time on these days: stocks that can be significantly higher in price five years from now. But which have had negative returns the past five years.

Finding weak performers among large-cap US stocks is much easier than many would believe. After all, they see this. 88% return on SPY the past five years. Outstanding.

What they probably don't see nearly as often is this, below. That's four index ETFs that invest in higher-dividend-yield stocks (DIV) (SPYD), small caps (IWM), or just the average stock of the top 1,000 in the US, including the biggies (EQAL).

Think about it this way. The biggest names have done so well, and the self-fulfilling prophecy around buying yesterday's winners has so fueled the S&P 500 index boom for so long, the rest are ignored. Not only are they ignored by many investors. They're also essentially non-entities in the major indexes.

As we sit here in early March 2026, the 20 largest S&P 500 stocks (TOPT) comprise just about 50% of the entire S&P 500 index. And 450 stocks make up the other 50%. That's an average weighting per stock among that 450 of...wait for it...0.11%!

Sorry, but in my book, they don't count. At least, to S&P 500 index investors. Yet so many still think the stock market is a "diversified basket" of stocks. In number, yes. In impact? No!

I'm more convinced every day that the current mega-cap bull run is essentially over. As I told my subscribers recently, stock market crashes occur in three phases. We are in phase 2. That was last month. I'm thinking we have progressed to maybe phase 2 1/3.

That means two things to me in my stock-picking activities:

1. Stocks are OK for trading, since that's what the market now rewards. Making 20% in 1-2 months on a single stock is easier than making a sustainable "double" over 4-5 years. That used to be very possible. In fact, I'm old enough to remember when some brokerage firms considered a buy rating to be a double within 4-5 years, about a 15-18% annualized return. But long-term investing in an era of algorithmic trading systems and S&P 500 index "passive inflows" from massive 401(k) plans has changed the game. Entirely so, I think.

2. There are still "long-term stock buys" but they are not as simple as buying a full position now and holding on for a half-decade or so. There's too much volatility, too many earnings misses/overnight disasters, and too little money backing of stocks as companies to own shares of.

The stock market is not entirely a casino. But you have to walk through the casino, maybe in circles for a while, losing your way a few times, before you finally come upon the part that isn't a casino. That's my characterization of today's stock market.

So, do I give up on stock-picking forever? No way! But I'm not jumping in based on "fundamentals" and "quality" and "terminal value" and things like that. I do not trust the stock market further than I can throw it, as the old metaphor goes.

But I'm a value buyer. So that prompts me to continuously be on the lookout for something, anything, that might eventually round into a long-term buy.

I'm a technician and a quant, not a fundamental analyst. Therefore, I lean on Seeking Alpha's Quant factor grades, particularly Valuation and Profitability, while also noting the Growth grades. Then, my technical analysis takes over. That's my self-proclaimed "super power" after charting since 1980, by paper and pencil, as a teenager, with my late father coaching me.

But charts for very long-term stock investing are less critical than how I use them for tactical investing and even my trading work. And with so much noise in stock price movement these days, ETFs often make for more confident charting.

So this article is also unlike many of my "usual" efforts. Because for the technical analysis, I'm just sticking some weekly charts at the end. The core is one of many stock screens I do regularly, in search of something that "moves me," and would make me want to go outside my bond ladder/tactical ETF allocation core portfolio zone. To find some stocks that might just be worth tracking for eventual ownership.

Spoiler alert: There will not be buy/sell/hold ratings at the end of this article. That's for the other 99% of writers. To me, the rating thing is so played out since doing so regularly would require a shift in view more often than we are even advised to cover a particular stock. I'm not one to see a 20-30% up move in a stock in a few months and say, "I'm a long-term holder," whereby it likely gives it all back, and then re-rallies. That's wasted time to me.

The five-year "losers" filter, using Seeking Alpha's screener and Quant factor ratings

Yes, the "setup" here was lengthy. Because we technician/quant types do not dwell on the narrative, the rationale, the whisper numbers for next quarter, or insider transaction trends. Bravo to those who do that and do it well. I'm a chartist. I just need to know I'm not charting trashy businesses. So I start with quant and filter as you see here:

I looked at large-cap stocks. Not exactly the full S&P 500, but in that neighborhood. Because this is a macro article, intended in large part to explain my broader views on stock selection in early 2026. And probably for quite a while.

What you see above are the results of a screen you can do yourself here. I started by saying, "Show me the stocks whose prices (including dividends) have not gone up over the past 5 years." Oh, there were many!

Next, I decided to eliminate any stock that was both a loser in price and a loser in valuation and/or profitability. So I insisted on both grades being at least B-.

And the growth grades were more of an additional checkpoint. I did not screen it, but I took note of it. I also noted market caps, and as you can see, the "losers bracket" I came up with had 21 stocks in all, ranging from market caps of $11 billion to more than $260 billion. But again, every one of these stocks, including many well-known names, is down in total return over the past five years. And over the past one to three years, I might add.

That focused me in on 21 names, but with an admitted bias toward those with at least non-awful growth grades. Lack of growth in this period of relative economic bliss, looking backward at least, is even less encouraging than it would be in a weak economy. It would signal to me that not only is the stock price going nowhere, but maybe the company is within a few years of a "going concern" issue. We all realize the economy is changing, with AI putting a lot of sacred cows in peril. As your favorite SaaS CEO.

Charting 21 stocks to find those with a fighting chance

That's the last step I performed in this study. I came up with five of them from that list of 21, and I briefly comment on them with their weekly price charts below. There was a sixth (BBY), which I will call an "honorable mention."

But frankly, none of these are very honorable. They're simply the output from a list that started with more than 500 US stocks, isolated the "low valuation/high profitability five-year total return losers," and lived to tell about it. Now, the five charts.

Adobe (ADBE)

Like I said, some ugly charts here. But at least this weekly view shows a shot for the stock to find an eventual bottom in the vicinity of its five-year low, which occurred back in late 2022. That's when the AI trade began, and tech stocks like this one flew higher.

Target (TGT)

Maybe the best of a bad bunch, technically speaking. There's at least some inertia off the recent $80 bottom, and earnings, just released this week, were good enough. Again, I'm no fundamental analyst, but I do wonder if part of the long-term incentive for investors here is that TGT, unlike WMT, has not really capitalized on its online ordering capability, as rival Walmart (WMT) has.

Workday (WDAY)

If I'm taking a small "flyer" position on any of these, it is this one. Perhaps those who really know the stock fundamentally can opine in the comments to this article. Same for any of the 21 on the list, frankly.

WDAY has the look of a stock that over the next 6-12 months either goes south of $100, where it has not been for an eternity, and back to $200, around where it cratered recently. Of course they're caught up in the AI replacement storm. But again, I'm not looking for bounces here. I have plenty of directions to look to for that.

As we technicians say, "Markets have a memory." So trading around its long-term price low and having just bounced off of $120, I'm at least taking note here.

Fidelity National Information Systems (FIS)

Not the household name that Boston-based Fidelity Financial is, this one is in a similar boat as some of the others above. Long-term bottom, given where it's priced now and the fact that it was there twice before during 2023? That year's price action shows both possibilities any time a stock's chart looks like this one.

Either it bounces/rallies briefly, suckers in a few people, and goes right back down. Or, it has a sustained run. "Sustained" as I described before. It doubles in 12 months, then proceeds to give it all back. I see this type of chart... all... the... time. And it is what colors my views on long-term single stock investing.

LyondellBasell Industries (LYB)

The last one is perhaps the one closest to busting loose to the upside, thanks in part to strong price support within its sector, the often-forgotten basic materials. LYB, however, can't lift the market on its own. It's not Nvidia (NVDA), after all.

And true to modern stock market form, it's already getting stretched/overvalued on a weekly chart basis. After just a 40% move since December of last year. This is what I mean. It has been a nice trade, a nice pop, for the daring. But is it a long-term buy and forget about it? Not yet.

Final thoughts for now

This was the analyst's version of cleaning up a college dorm room! But at least there's the start of a watchlist, to see how these develop. In hopes that one day I can buy them with more than a token position size. And once again do what many of us remember fondly. Buy stocks to hold them for doubles, triples, and then some.

data.title: Vermilion Energy: A Solid Q4 2025 And 2026 Looks Bright
data.description:
Overview

Vermilion Energy (VET)(VET:CA) is an oil & natural gas producer that is listed in both the U.S. and Canada. The company has most of its production coming from Canada, but there are also high-margin assets internationally. Vermilion has lately become more focused on natural gas production but also has oil-focused assets in France and Australia. The company reports in Canadian dollars, which is denoted C$ throughout the article.

I have covered Vermilion quarterly over the last few years and my prior articles can be found here. Today's article will focus on the Q4 2025 results that were released yesterday after the close. All my 2025 articles on the company were rated strong buys, but we have seen a rally of around 50% from those levels last year, so even if I remain bullish on Vermilion, I have lowered the rating to a buy.

The stock price of Vermilion has, over the last few years, underperformed many peers, which is, in my view, mostly due to the stock being out of favor. The valuation has become more attractive than many peers, partly due to having more financial leverage, which is rarely celebrated when energy prices weaken. The diversified production profile has possibly also been a drag on performance, even if I like the company's asset mix.

With all that said, over the last 6 months we have seen the stock price of Vermilion outperform substantially, being up 58% during the period. The recent outperformance is likely due to a combination of factors. The valuation of Vermilion was very depressed 6 months ago, the higher financial leverage has likely provided a boost when energy prices have improved, and natural gas prices have done reasonably well during this period as well.

Q4 2025 Results

Vermilion reported 121,308 boe/d in production during Q4 2025, which was a 2% increase compared to Q3 2025, and a 45% increase compared to Q4 2024. The growth in production over the last year is primarily due to the acquisition of Westbrick Energy in early 2025. International production has been somewhat stable around 30,000 boe/d lately.

Production is expected to be roughly flat in 2026 as well, with guidance being in the 118,000-122,000 boe/d range, with 70% coming from natural gas. Capital expenditure is expected to be around C$615M in 2026.

The funds flow from the North American assets saw a healthy 33% increase quarter-over-quarter, to C$13/boe in Q4 2025. The international funds flow was marginally down quarter-over-quarter and came in at C$48/boe during the most recent quarter.

We can, in the second chart below, see that the international natural gas production in Ireland, Germany, the Netherlands, and Croatia has consistently been generating funds flow above C$40/boe, and Q4 2025 was no exception.

Vermilion reported a funds flow of C$241M in Q4, down 5% compared to the prior quarter. The main reason for the quarter-over-quarter decline was a lower hedging gain in Q4 compared to Q3. This was still a relatively good quarterly result when we consider how soft oil prices were during the quarter.

The quarterly dividend is now C$0.135 per quarter, up slightly compared to 2025, and it equates to a dividend yield of 3.4%. There have also been buybacks over the last few years, even if the company has been more focused on deleveraging in 2025. The net debt to last twelve months' funds flow is still somewhat elevated compared to many peers at 1.3, even if that is far from extreme.

Vermilion provided a reserve update in the Q4 2025 financials, where total proved developed producing ("PDP") reserves were 210 MMboe, which was a 25% increase compared to last year. Total proved plus probable ("2P") reserves increase 36% to 592 MMboe, which is a reserve life index of 14 years. The company also thinks the international reserve numbers are somewhat conservative.

The lack of reserves has been a criticism of Vermilion in the past, and while most of these reserves have come from the Westbrick Energy acquisition, there should be fewer concerns about the reserves going forward.

The company did also announce a C$572M non-cash impairment expense during Q4 2025, which is far from ideal. This was on its assets in Australia, France, and Ireland due to lower prices used for forecasting. This is not a major concern in my view, though, as I think a Brent Crude price of around $80/bbl is extremely conservative 5-10 years out.

Conclusion

Using the latest share price and Q4 2025 financials, Vermilion has a market cap of C$2.4B and an enterprise value of C$3.8B. The forward-looking EV to EBITDA ratio has come up from its lowest level in 2025, but we are still at historically low levels, with the ratio now being 3.7. This is an attractive valuation multiple compared to its history and peers. The EBITDA estimates are also very likely to be conservative if current energy prices persist for much of 2026.

The main risks with an investment in Vermilion are the commodity price risk and its financial leverage. The production is relatively well diversified between regions and products, but softer energy prices would no doubt have a negative impact on the stock price. The company has a good amount of hedges in place for 2026 and 2027 as well. I also expect the company to be adding further hedges now that energy prices have increased quite a bit compared to what we have seen in the last year.

The financial leverage is on the higher side compared to many peers, but a net debt to conservative funds flow of 1.3 is not overly concerning in my view. It is also worth remembering that higher financial leverage can provide a boost if current energy prices persist for an extended period, where the company will be generating excess returns. I rate Vermilion as a buy at the current level, and I am long the stock.

data.title: SPYD: Overlooked 4% Yielding ETF That Could Benefit From The Growth To Value Rotation
data.description:
Introduction

In recent years, I've become more and more accustomed to owning ETFs as part of a diversified portfolio. As someone who once scoffed at the idea of owning them, ETFs have grown on me.

Although they usually have diversified portfolios, many have performed well against the overall market. This makes them attractive as core holdings and allows you to build individual positions around them.

If you're an investor who prioritizes income, then the State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD) may be a good fit inside a diversified portfolio.

In this article, I break down SPYD's portfolio, historical performance, and why I'm initiating a buy rating on this ETF.

Who Is SPYD?

SPYD was created on October 21, 2015, by State Street, the same advisors who created the U.S.'s first ETF, State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), in 1993.

SPYD was designed to track the performance of the 80 highest-yielding companies in the index. What I specifically like about this ETF, aside from the higher yield at 4.11%, is its low-cost structure.

The expense ratio sits at just 0.07%, making them competitive with popular dividend-focused funds like Schwab's U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) & iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO).

SCHD has the lowest expense ratio at 0.06%, while DGRO's is the highest of the group at 0.08%. All three are very reasonable for passively managed funds. For SPYD, an investor would pay $7 for every $10,000 invested.

On a positive note, SPYD has the highest yield of the three. SCHD's sits at 3.31%, while DGRO's is the lowest at 1.97%.

This is likely due to the latter's significantly higher exposure to the Technology (XLK) sector and the fact that SPYD prioritizes higher-yielding equities.

As a result, this increases the likelihood for SPYD to see lower growth than its two peers. But again, SPYD should be held for income with the potential to see capital appreciation.

Here you can see the ETF has underperformed the S&P in the past 5 years in total returns, up 60% to 89% for the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO).

The past 3 years have been more of the same, with the gap widening a bit, likely due to the large-cap stocks outperforming due to AI.

But in the past 6 months, SPYD has actually managed to outperform the VOO in total returns due to investors rotating from large-cap stocks to value. While this is no guarantee going forward, AI fears, particularly in software, could enable SPYD to continue to outperform over the near to medium term.

Top Holdings

SPYD's top holdings include well-known names like Verizon (VZ), which has been on a tear lately. Peer AT&T (T) is also a top holding that has performed well in the past year, up over 7% at the time of writing.

It shouldn't be a surprise that Iron Mountain (IRM), a REIT that has performed well in the past year, is also a top holding. For some investors, this may be a turn-off, as many REITs have underperformed in recent years due to higher-for-longer interest rates.

Investors should be aware of REIT sensitivity to short- and long-term interest rates. Volatility in these could result in underperformance. Actually, Real Estate (XLRE) makes up SPYD's top sector at more than 25%.

Consumer Staples (XLP) and Financials (XLF) round out the top three. You can see the ETF has very little exposure to the Technology sector at just 2.33%. So, again, this ETF should be held strictly for those who are looking to prioritize income.

For context, we take a look at how SPYD's high exposure to REITs fared during the rate hike cycle from March of 2022 until August of 2023. VOO was the only ETF in the green during that time frame.

SCHD & DGRO were in the red, but modestly at approximately 4% and less than 1%, respectively. SPYD was down over 11%, showing the impact REITs had on its portfolio.

Although interest rates have come down, the recent attack on Iran could result in inflation spiking again.

If so, this could cause the FED to raise interest rates, although I think this scenario is unlikely. But investors should be aware of this, as SPYD would likely underperform as a result.

A good thing is SPYD rebalances twice a year in January and July, so it could decrease its exposure to the sector.

At the end of 2025, companies like CVS Health Corp. (CVS) & Ford Motor Company (F), both of whom have faced their fair share of headwinds in recent years.

ONEOK (OKE), also a favorite amongst income-focused investors, is a top holding with 25 years of dividend stability. Although the stock is down double digits in the past year, they raised their dividend by 4% back in January to $1.07 a share.

I, personally, do like funds with more of a balance and higher exposure to the Technology sector for the growth piece.

I also look for funds with higher exposure to the Healthcare (XLV) sector in case of an economic downturn, as these historically perform well in times of economic uncertainty.

Dividends & NAV Growth

Since inception, SPYD's distributions have been a bit volatile. But these have grown 29% from $1.5139 in 2016 to $1.957 in 2025. Their latest declared dividend was for $0.5493 back in December. The ETF pays on a quarterly basis.

Because the fund holds high exposure to the Real Estate sector, their distributions are less tax favorable. According to their Prospectus, distributions are considered ordinary income, making them better suited for retirees with tax-advantaged accounts.

NAV growth has also been solid since inception with a growth rate of 9.05%. And although the fund seems to be overlooked when compared to other dividend-focused peers, their performance since inception has been solid as well.

In the past year, SPYD has lagged both SCHD & DGRO in price returns. In my opinion, this has more to do with the popularity of both funds as investors have rotated from growth to value stocks recently.

In total returns over the past 3 years, SPYD has kept up with SCHD, trailing them by less than 5%.

But over a 10-year period, both SCHD & DGRO have significantly outperformed SPYD. However, the latter is still solid with an annualized total return of nearly 16%.

Why SPYD Could Do Well Going Forward

While I don't expect SPYD to outperform peers like SCHD & DGRO due to their higher Real Estate exposure, less popularity, and minimal exposure to Tech, I do think they could see some solid price appreciation if the rotation continues.

The reason why is that we are likely to see multiple rate cuts starting in June due to the President's new FED chair. As a result, lower base rates will lead to lower rates on fixed-rate investments.

And I believe this will benefit the fund as investors look to rotate back into the market looking for higher yields. While dividend stocks have outperformed recently, due to the potential to see lower in the back half of the year, I think the rally could continue.

Moreover, SPYD has higher exposure to Consumer Staples & Financials, two sectors likely to benefit from the AI. And they are also currently much cheaper in comparison to SCHD & DGRO on a P/E and P/B basis.

Fund

P/E

P/B

SPYD

14.93x

1.84x

SCHD

18.66x

3.46x

DGRO

23.93x

3.94x

Risks & Conclusion

While I continue to think SPYD is a solid fund, there's a likelihood it will continue to underperform vs other popular dividend-focused ETFs.

Their high exposure to Real Estate is likely a turn-off with their distributions considered ordinary income.

They are also at higher risk of underperformance should we see volatility in long-term treasuries once base rates decline. However, I do consider them a solid fund for anyone looking to prioritize income and who already has a diversified portfolio.

With a yield above 4% and solid total returns over the past decade, State Street's S&P High Dividend ETF may be a fund to consider going forward.

data.title: IGR: NAV Erosion Likely To Continue Unless Dividends Are Reduced
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the CBRE Global Real Estate Income Fund (IGR), I issued a hold rating due to the questionable dividend coverage and uncertainty around interest rates. While the rest of the market sells off, real estate has seen a bit of a revival in the last few months. Additionally, IGR has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period, which prompted me to reassess the fund's value proposition, performance, and outlook for 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that IGR's share price has declined by nearly 7.2%. However, the fund has experienced some positive momentum over the last two months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 7.5% over the same time frame. IGR now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 14.6% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. However, I believe that the generous distributions are harmful to the overall sustainability of the fund. IGR would benefit from a distribution cut because it would help improve the NAV growth.

The recent rise in share price has resulted in IGR now trading at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range. Therefore, valuation is a bit tricky at this time since the underlying earnings haven't improved year-over-year. Although interest rate cuts may be a catalyst at some point, I don't think this is the best time for long-term investors to accumulate shares. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy that IGR implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, IGR has total managed assets of $1B that are spread across 81 different holdings. The fund's primary objective is to provide a high level of current income from a global range of real estate securities. However, the fund is unique because it combines traditional common stocks with preferred stock to achieve a blend of capital appreciation and income generation over time.

The fund is most heavily allocated to U.S.-based securities while offering meaningful exposure to global securities across different regions of the globe. Approximately 60.7% of assets are focused on the U.S., with the remaining portion of its portfolio being spread across regions of Europe, Asia, and Australia.

Expanding on this diversity, IGR maintains an efficient allocation across the different sub-sectors of real estate. Therefore, IGR can avoid any sort of concentration risk to specific focus areas. As we can see below, IGR is most heavily allocated to retail REITs at 14.70% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrial and healthcare securities, which accounts for 14.03% and 13.18% of assets, respectively.

When looking at the top positions of the fund, we can see many notable names. The fund is also slightly top-heavy since the top ten positions make up more than a third of total assets. Welltower (WELL) is now the largest holding at 6.25%. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable names, including Equinix (EQIX), American Tower (AMT), Simon Property Group (SPG), and Crown Castle (CCI), just to name a few.

The fund also utilizes leverage as a way to amplify its earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently amounts to 31.82% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$300M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this high interest rate environment because leverage can amplify risks. For instance, leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that decline in value. Since interest rates are elevated, the cost of debt may be negatively impacting the fund's operating spread. Despite several cuts throughout 2025, interest rates still remain near the higher end of their ten-year range.

Updated Valuation & Performance Insights

When I previously covered IGR, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 1.5%. Following the small price rally over the last month, IGR now trades at a premium to NAV of 2.08%. For reference, IGR has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.94% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, IGR now trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range. Therefore, I don't believe this is the best time to accumulate shares for long-term investors.

Since my last coverage, IGR has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. This is the most up-to-date report that provides us with transparency into the fund's earnings and financial highlights. Net investment income amounted to $0.09 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.03 per share. Looking back on prior years, IGR's net investment income levels have been low ever since interest rates were raised to their decade highs. This could imply that the fund's operating expenses are heavily impacting the operating spread for IGR. Additionally, the low investment income means that IGR is heavily dependent on net realized gains to make up for this lower income.

The only challenge is that net realized gains have also been suppressed and wildly varied in this current interest rate environment. So despite market indices racing to all-time highs during 2025, IGR's net realized gains of only $0.03 per share weren't quite a reflection of the overall market strength. This variability makes it very hard to predict how the fund will perform during any given period. Furthermore, this reliance on net realized gains can negatively impact the trajectory of the NAV over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $10.48 per share
  • 2022: $6.31 per share
  • 2023: $6.20 per share
  • 2024: $5.10 per share
  • 2025: $4.50 per share

As we can see, the fund's NAV has continued to decline year over year, which is a clear indication that something is wrong. Since the fund continues to pay out more than it actually earns, the underlying NAV and share price will continue to erode over time. When looking at the long-term performance of the fund, we can see that IGR's share price has declined by more than 34%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 81.6% over the same time frame. So while investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the distributions that are paid out to shareholders, this is an instance where the distribution may actually be harmful to the long-term success of the fund.

Furthermore, the focus on income generation may translate to underperformance over a longer holding period, especially if those distributions aren't supported by earnings. A good example of this can be demonstrated when comparing IGR's performance against a more traditional sector-tracking ETF, such as the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE). Over the last ten years, XLRE has provided a total return of 102.8%, which outperforms IGR over the same time frame. Since XLRE doesn't prioritize income generation, its positions are capable of growing over time without disruption, whereas IGR has to sell positions in order to support distributions.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.06 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.72 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, it is clear that IGR struggles to generate the necessary earnings to support the distributions that are being paid. However, IGR decided to raise dividends back in 2022 and keep these payouts elevated. Although the fund isn't able to support these distributions, I believe this was strategically done as a way to shrink the fund's discount to NAV valuation.

Now that IGR trades in the premium-to-NAV territory, I anticipate that a healthy distribution cut may need to be implemented as a way to bring the fund back in alignment with success. A large distribution cut of 50% would still provide investors with a competitive dividend yield of ~7.3% and would relieve the fund's pressure to produce higher earnings. A lower payout would increase the probability that IGR will produce excess cash flow that can help change the trajectory of the NAV momentum. If IGR decides not to reduce the distributions, the NAV will continue to erode over time and negatively impact returns for shareholders.

Lastly, we can get confirmation of IGR's generous distribution policy by referring to the latest Section 19(a) notice. This confirms that 91% of the most recent distribution was classified as return of capital, which means that IGR has not generated enough earnings to support distributions, so they are being funded directly from the NAV. While these distributions may be more tax-efficient, they can be harmful for the fund and cause erosion when heavily depended upon for a long stretch of time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on IGR at this time. The fund provides you with direct exposure to some of the highest quality REITs in the world, but the underlying structure is inefficient. The fund's earnings have remained suppressed in this high interest rate environment, so the fund has struggled to generate earnings that can actually support payouts. As long as IGR pays out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will continue to erode over time. This will limit shareholder returns compared to more standard sector-tracking ETFs like XLRE. Therefore, I do not believe this is an appropriate time to accumulate just yet.

data.title: Meta: Time To Sit On The Fence (Downgrade)
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc.’s (META) share price has rallied notably since my previous bullish call until late January, when the stock suffered a post-earnings selloff due to the panic related to aggressively increasing AI-related CAPEX. Thus, META currently trades almost at exactly the same price as it did in mid-December.

As a Meta investor, I am also quite concerned about the company’s plans to aggressively ramp up investments in data centers. The company’s Q4 earnings were stellar, but I think that with a significant increase in capital investments, Meta’s historically strong ROIC is at risk. The management sees potential to improve value for digital advertising clients by adding new AI-powered features for improved targeting, but the competition in generative AI models has become so intense, and Meta doesn’t look like the most powerful one in this domain. Meta's digital advertising business is already quite mature, and it is difficult to expect skyrocketing profitability from the legacy segment because of its scale and likely increasing competition for marketing budgets with LLMs. All in all, I am not buying more META shares and believe that it is a hold at the moment.

Recent developments

Meta released its latest quarterly earnings on January 28th, delivering another dual beat against consensus estimates. Revenue growth was solid during the quarter with a 23.8% YoY increase, while the adjusted EPS expanded from $8.02 to $8.88. It was a quality EPS expansion, as it was underpinned by almost a 10% increase in EBITDA, which reflects improved operating efficiency.

While Q4 performance was extremely strong with big positive surprises in terms of both revenue and EPS, there is an elephant in the room, which is the management’s initiative to significantly increase capital investments in FY2026. Meta’s annual CAPEX already reached almost $70 billion in FY2025, which is almost a five-fold increase compared to FY2020. And even this huge annual CAPEX of $70 billion last year is far from peak, because the management said during the earnings call that they expect FY2026 CAPEX to be between $115 billion and $135 billion. The midpoint is $125 billion, which is a 79% increase compared to FY2025 and a 3.4x increase compared to FY2024.

One of the reasons why I have been investing in Meta for several years is because of the company’s extremely high average return on capital employed [ROIC] of almost 23% over the last decade. The difference between return on invested capital and cost of capital is pure shareholders’ value, and Meta has been historically very strong in maintaining a wide window between these two metrics. With all that being said, Meta's profits must grow in line with its rapidly growing investments in fixed assets. Capital employed is total assets minus current liabilities, and we can see that this amount has increased significantly over the last few years.

The above table suggests that Meta's capital employed increased by 123% over the last five years. Given that the management plans to increase investments in CAPEX by $125 billion in FY2026, it will be quite reliable to say that capital employed will increase by at least $100 billion over the next year. In relative terms, it will be approximately a 30% increase in capital employed. Therefore, to prevent ROIC compression, Meta's operating profit should also demonstrate the same growth in FY2026. I am saying "operating profit" instead of NOPAT [net operating profit after tax] for the purposes of this article for simplicity purposes.

I think that such an increase in the operating profit is highly unlikely because Wall Street analysts expect Meta's EPS to be approximately flat in 2026 compared to 2025. Therefore, ROIC compression looks inevitable by the end of FY2026 at least.

Moreover, there is a notable fundamental risk for Meta that the global digital advertising market can be disrupted by generative AI companies. The global digital advertising market is huge and rapidly growing, expected to hit almost $2 trillion over the next decade.

Generative AI companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) are also investing hundreds of billions in compute power, and expanding into the digital advertising market looks like an extremely reasonable step considering the large user bases for their flagship LLMs. For example, Sam Altman said a few weeks ago that his company might replicate Instagram-style ads in ChatGPT. Besides these two giant pure-gen AI plays, there is also Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). It is a real monster, which is already the global digital advertising leader, rapidly developing its own generative AI model, Gemini.

Of course, Meta is also developing its own LLMs, but it is behind the aforementioned players in terms of market share. Therefore, there is an increasing risk of Meta losing the race for marketing budgets of clients to generative AI models.

Valuation update

Meta is down by approximately 1% YTD, meaning that its market cap remains at the $1.6 trillion level. Meta looks perfectly fairly valued at the moment, as its current multiples are very close to historical averages across the board. Moreover, the PEG ratio of 1.08 is almost identical to the sector median. Therefore, based on valuation ratios, it appears that there is not much upside left on the table after the share price has sextupled since late 2022 lows.

If we take the consensus EPS forecast for FY2026 of $30.24 and multiply it by the historical forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 22.7, we get the target price of $686.5. This is approximately 5% higher than the actual share price, which is a very limited upside potential. I think that using Wall Street’s EPS forecast for FY2026 is a reliable approach because Meta very rarely misses consensus earnings forecasts.

Risks to my rating downgrade

While my valuation update suggests that Meta is approximately fairly valued, Wall Street analysts disagree with me, as their consensus target price is $863. This means a solid 31.8% upside potential from current levels, which might add a lot of optimism to the market. Therefore, analysts price new big positive surprises or potential earnings revisions, which is always a possible scenario when we speak about a stellar company like Meta.

While I have doubts about surprises from growth potential generated by organic and in-house initiatives, there might be some moves from the M&A front. It is a possible scenario because Meta’s growth strategy is multi-faceted and the company makes strategic acquisitions sometimes. There is a proven record of extremely successful acquisitions [Instagram, WhatsApp], and the company’s balance sheet is rock-solid, with more than $80 billion in cash and extremely low debt relative to Meta’s $1.6 trillion market cap.

A potential acquisition of an already profitable company will highly likely add to positive long-term EPS revisions, which will inevitably result in positive repricing. On the other hand, over the short term the stock price usually dips after acquisition news or even rumors.

Bottom line

To conclude, I am downgrading Meta to a neutral Hold rating because I am also very concerned about the company’s ability to maintain its historically stellar ROIC after all these huge investments in AI infrastructure. Moreover, valuation analysis suggests that there is almost no upside left on the table.

data.title: Sabesp: A Successful Privatization Story, But Less Asymmetric Now
data.description:
In my last coverage of Sabesp (SBS), I reiterated a “Strong Buy” rating. In doing so, the Brazilian ADR has done very well, accumulating total returns in excess of 50%, counting dividends up to the time of writing.

In doing so, I recognized the bull case from the company being a newly privatized state-owned company in Brazil (in this case, in mid-2024). At the time, the state of São Paulo in Brazil owned 50.3% of the company, and post-privatization, the stake fell to 18.3%, and it was no longer the controlling shareholder.

When the company was state-owned, decisions tended to be slow and dependent on politics. After privatization, the expectation was for cost-cutting initiatives and more professional management. And this would eventually have an impact on margins and return on capital for shareholders.

Along with the privatization (where around R$14.7 billion was raised), came a giant investment plan, "CapEx", to commit R$70 billion to universalize water and sewage treatment—which still does not serve all families in Brazil's main state.

Each investment goes into the RAB “Regulated Asset Base,” which basically determines how much return the regulator allows the company to earn through tariffs. Of course, sanitation is a regulated sector. The allowed return (regulatory WACC) is usually around ~8% on the asset base.

So basically, the engine of Sabesp's thesis comes from the RAB amplified by the regulatory return yielding profits. I go over what has happened since my last take on the company—almost a year ago—to update why it's still worth being constructive on SBS even with the recent strong valuation.

Privatization Is Beginning To Show Operational Results

As expected, Sabesp's CapEx really accelerated post-privatization. The company reported R$10.4 billion in the first 9 months of 2025—up 151% vs. 9M24 and R$13.2 billion ttm. Investments in sewage grew by +240% and water by +46%, showing that the R$70 billion of CapEx planned has started to come to fruition.

This is reflected in the expansion of the customer base, with 948,000 units with sewage treatment, 733,000 new units with sewage, and 616,000 new units with water—with 148,000 new connections added in 3Q25 alone, the last quarter reported. This is proof that infrastructure expansion has generated physical system growth.

Perhaps more importantly, the long-awaited post-privatization efficiencies are already showing up in costs. OpEx fell 16% 9M25 vs. 9M24, a direct implication of the reduction in personnel (-12% YoY in 3Q25) and better collection of water and sewage bills.

And the icing on the cake is that even with the massive investment, adjusted EBITDA is rising. Sabesp posted in Q3 R$3.2 billion in Adj. EBITDA, up 14.7% YoY and an Adj. EBITDA margin of 59% vs. 51% in my period last year. Also, the balance sheet looks pretty solid with cash and financial investments at R$11.5 billion vs. R$3.5 billion in the same period last year, with total assets up to R$96 billion from R$76.1 billion—basically pure RAB growth.

The central idea is that with Sabesp able to report EBITDA close to R$12-14 billion annualized, it is possible to finance part of the CapEx with its own cash flow.

Revisiting Sabesp’s Value Creation Through RoRAB

It seems quite evident that the original thesis of investments accelerating efficiency is being well executed, and the strong performance of the ADR subscribes to that.

But again, in the midst of the ADR's strong rally, is this the time to take the plunge and realize gains?

In my latest coverage, I evaluated SBS value generation capacity through the lens of Return on Regulated Asset Base, i.e., “RoRAB”—which is basically the operating profit “EBIT” divided by the RAB. This metric is preferable due to the regulatory model that involves tariff issues and fixed regulatory returns. In April last year, I arrived at a RoRAB of 18.3% for Sabesp—about twice the regulatory WACC.

Now, one year later, the RAB is reaching roughly R$84.3 billion (R$95.9 billion in total assets minus R$4.9 billion in cash and R$6.6 billion in financial investments), and based on trailing EBIT of R$10.3 billion, I arrive at an updated RoRAB of about 12.3%. But again, keep in mind that water and sewage are a regulated sector in Brazil, and there is a regulatory WACC of ~8%. That being said, a RoRAB above this level still implies substantial value creation—just not as much above the regulatory return as it was in April last year.

Moving on to valuations, taking into account the R$130.35 billion of enterprise value that SBS trades at, this would equate to an EV/RAB of 1.55x—also above the 1.22x that served as a pillar for my "Strong Buy" rating issued in April last year. In other words, clearly the market has already recognized and reassessed the multiple of the regulated base.

Lastly, here's an update on the RAB projection based on a new base. The R$84 billion RAB, when considering this year's R$15 billion CapEx guidance, and over the next five years (2030) reaching R$75 billion, would result in a future RAB of ~R$160 billion, very close to my original model of R$167 billion, showing that the thesis seems to be well calibrated.

A Brief Word on Earnings

As Sabesp's earnings are just around the corner (the company is expected to post its fourth quarter results later on March 16th), consensus is working under expectations of a revenue decrease of 19% YoY and an EPS increase of +49% YoY. The explanation, however, for such seemingly weak top-line growth is due to 4Q25 coming from a distorted accounting comparison base and a mix between items recognized below the operating line vs. the revenue line—as the company noted in its 3Q25 earnings call.

On the EPS side, even if revenues come in weaker shape, tied either to the comparative base or accounting distortions, the bottom line should still come in strong given the improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin driven by post-privatization efficiency enhancements, especially regarding headcount reduction.

Rather than the top and bottom line numbers, investors will likely be looking for the CapEx trajectory and management's narrative towards its capacity to sustain the investment pace required to meet universalization targets. I believe that CapEx coming in at a range of R$4 billion to 4.5 billion in the fourth quarter—pretty much in line with the actual R$15 billion annual guidance—would represent that the original R$70 million investment plan through 2029 is moving accordingly, and ensure the steady expansion of the RAB, which ultimately supports future tariff adjustments and long-term growth. CapEx numbers below the yearly guidance would likely raise questions in terms of management's execution capacity and the attainment of universalization targets.

Risks And Final Thoughts

Sabesp's biggest risk is the regulatory landscape, especially since 2026 is an election year not only for the President, but also for Congress, the Senate, and the government of every state in Brazil. The implication of lower tariffs and possible political pressure to reduce water tariffs tends to impact revenue and return on RAB directly for the thesis. In December last year, Sabesp went through its first post-privatization tariff review, and the average water tariff was adjusted for inflation and came to ~R$6.7/m³—while in the state model it would have come to R$7.36 m³—which I consider a victory for the company.

That being said, I believe that although Sabesp's thesis has been going well, much of the original asymmetry has closed, and so I wouldn't expect 2026 to be a year of such robust returns as 2025 was. However, as Sabesp continues to report a RoRAB above the regulatory WACC, this still signals a very welcome generation of value.

For this reason, I remain constructive, although more aware of the asymmetry, believing that SBS remains a thesis to be pursued in the long term based on the clear improvement in efficiency post-privatization, which will tend to provide attractive returns to its shareholders both in terms of dividends and added value.
